news_summary: Scientists are increasingly embracing poetry to foster creativity and emotional connection in their work.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques se tournent de plus en plus vers la poésie pour stimuler la créativité et favoriser la connexion émotionnelle dans leur travail.
news_summary_es: Los científicos recurren cada vez más a la poesía para fomentar la creatividad y la conexión emocional en su trabajo.

articles.title: Engaging the head and the heart: why scientists turn to poetry
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 06 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01028-3Some researchers use verse to visualize complex problems or translate the wonders of science for wider audiences.
articles.content:
Researcher-poets use verse to help to ease isolation and grief, sing the praises of nature or depict the joys of commuting by bicycle.Credit: WeBond Creations/Getty

Last year, in a moving family farewell celebration, palliative-care physician and researcher Danielle Chammas said goodbye to a long-time patient with cancer. When Chammas came home afterwards, she wrote a poem. Her Defiance, which was later published in the journal JAMA Oncology, describes the woman as “the leaf clinging fiercely to the tree” (D. Chammas JAMA Oncol. 12, 215; 2026).

With family at bedside,/ and the beau’s guitar/ filling the room,/ the leaf released the tree,/ and she knew,/ for the briefest of moments,/ what it meant to fly.

How a self-taught biologist transformed nature writing — and inspired Darwin

Chammas, who works at the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF), says her patient “would have walked through fire to stay in her daughter’s life” and didn’t let cancer dictate how she was going to die.

Chammas also co-directs the Poetic Medicine programme at the UCSF MERI Center for Humanity in Healthcare. The centre researches the art of poem-making to help with grief and “aims to cultivate spaces where voices are valued, connections are made, and healing is nurtured”, she explains. At weekly online meetings, clinicians, carers, patients and members of the public are invited to listen to poems and write their own. Participants join from around the world. Chammas often shares the works of poets such as the US writer Maya Angelou and the thirteenth-century Persian mystic Rumi with people in her care.

“The humanities are foundational to my ability to do my clinical work,” Chammas says, because poetry enables her to “truly accompany somebody through something unthinkable”. That process, she says, is as fundamental to her work “as my knowledge of how opiates hit pain-modulating mu receptors”.

Science and poetry might seem like an odd pairing. One deals in facts; the other, seemingly, in feelings. But the two fields don’t negate each other, Chammas says. “We live in a very dualistic and binary culture, good or bad, healthy or sick, living or dying, optimists or pessimists. But I think we do ourselves and our society a real disservice if that’s our only way of seeing the world.” Poetry taps into “the multitudes that are in us” and is “a really beautiful way to open our minds”, she says.

Indeed, physicians and scientists can be poets, too. For instance, the physician William Carlos Williams and the immunologist Miroslav Holub wrote and published highly praised poems. The mathematician Ada Lovelace, widely recognized as an inventor of computer programming, also invented her own genre, called poetical science, which brings together abstract mathematics and the imagination. Of the Analytical Engine, a mechanical computing machine conceived by Charles Babbage in the 1830s, Lovelace wrote that it “weaves algebraic patterns just as the Jacquard loom weaves flowers and leaves”.

Like Lovelace, poetry-writing researchers and physicians interviewed by Nature’s careers team describe how poetry can meld seamlessly with science. Many draw inspiration from scientific phenomena and nature, such as fish, hurricanes, the climate crisis and even electricity pylons. They aim to translate the wonder and beauty of the world of tiny molecules and the one visible around us into words. By pouring their scientific knowledge into poetic forms, they often find fresh ways of understanding complex problems and developing solutions.

Scribing science

Colleen Farrelly is a mathematician at Post Urban Ventures, a firm specializing in artificial intelligence and deep tech in Miami, Florida. She echoes Lovelace in seeing mathematical patterns in poetic structures, “especially in avant-garde poetry, where we’re creating collages of images”. Farrelly specializes in topology, a branch of maths that delves into the properties of structures. For instance, a sphere can be pulled into an ellipsoid by stretching it, yet its geometric properties stay the same.

Black holes, love and poetry — an artistic exploration of intimacy and adventure

She uses poetry to organize her thoughts and make sense of her experiences. One example is a poem she composed last September while two swirling storms, hurricanes Humberto and Imelda, orbited around each other about 800 kilometres off the coast of Florida. Published in the poetry magazine Rattle, Weathering the Storm describes the Fujiwhara effect, a phenomenon first described in 1921 by Japanese meteorologist Sakuhei Fujiwhara in which two storms dance around each other.

In the words of Farrelly’s poem,

The Fujiwhara effect suggests that storm systems in close proximity feed off each other’s energy unpredictably. Two systems might clash or combine or canoodle until bursting apart like twin flames.

“It’s great to communicate things in poetry, because I don’t think a lot of people know about the Fujiwhara effect,” says Farrelly. “I really like being able to explain maths to lay audiences that way.” She adds that opening up this creative side is “so useful in science”.

That resonates with Fionn Rogan, an engineer at the Sustainability Institute at University College Cork in Ireland. He works on ‘transformative pathways’ for moving Ireland’s energy systems away from fossil fuels to renewable sources such as solar and wind power. To address such complicated challenges, Rogan says, “you have to engage the head and the heart, and I find poetry is a good way to bridge those two”.

Poetry, he says, “is about looking seriously at the world and seeing what’s right in front of you, but you’ve forgotten to notice”. Rogan often finds himself concocting short, three-line haiku poems in his head as he cycles to and from his home in Cork to the university, a commute of about 10 kilometres each way. Some of his poems address people’s unsustainable driving habits. For instance, in one of his favourite haiku, he wrote,

If I saw myself/ As some motorists see me/ I wouldn’t see me

Many of his haiku and haibun — a type of poem that interweaves prose with haiku — address the climate footprint of travel and the mental-health benefits of cycling past “buses, cars, vans queuing in abundance”.

Rogan hosts a podcast on renewable-energy poetry. He is compiling an anthology of such poems by other scribes that find beauty in the “technological sublime, the feelings of awe we get when we look at huge human-made pieces of technology”. Both poetry and engineering share “a similar aesthetic of economy”, he explains. “An elegant solution or an elegant equation can be a kind of poem.”

‘That’s funny’: creative solutions for time-starved researchers

One of the poems that Rogan selected for his energy collection is In Praise of Pylons by Victoria Gatehouse, a clinical operations manager at ICON, a biotechnology research firm in Dublin. Gatehouse, who lives on the outskirts of Halifax, UK, on the edge of the Yorkshire moors, is the author of The Hawthorn Bride (2024), a collection of poems “rooted in a Yorkshire landscape of crags, moorland, reservoirs and woods”. She also wrote a forthcoming poetry book for children, Aardvark Day, which centres around quirky animals such as a photosynthetic sea slug known as a leaf sheep (Costasiella kuroshimae) and the bioluminescent deep-sea jellyfish (Atolla wyvillei).

“I am a lyric poet, so my aim is to make things beautiful or give a sense of awe and wonder,” Gatehouse says. In her poem about the electric pylons, inspired by the “strange sympathy” she felt for these majestic sentinels on the moors, she wrote,
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the inspiring integration of poetry and science to foster emotional healing, enhance public understanding of complex scientific concepts, and promote environmental awareness. It demonstrates a meaningful, broad impact on society by bridging humanities and science, supporting mental health, and encouraging creative communication of important issues like climate change and renewable energy. The story is focused, detailed, and shows tangible benefits beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: Scientists use poetry to share science and support healing
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some scientists and doctors are turning to poetry to express feelings, explain complex ideas, and connect with others. This approach combines science with art to help people understand and cope with difficult experiences.

**What happened** – Danielle Chammas, a doctor and researcher at the University of California, San Francisco, wrote a poem after saying goodbye to a patient with cancer. She also leads a program where patients, caregivers, and medical staff write and share poems to help with grief and healing. Other scientists, like mathematician Colleen Farrelly and engineer Fionn Rogan, create poems inspired by storms, energy, and nature to explain scientific ideas and raise awareness about climate issues.

**Impact** – Poetry gives scientists a new way to communicate complicated topics like math patterns or climate change in a way that many people can understand and feel. It also helps doctors support patients emotionally during hard times. This blending of science and poetry opens minds and hearts, making science more relatable and encouraging thoughtful care.

**What's next step** – Programs combining poetry and science are growing, with more researchers using poems to share their work and feelings. Collections of poems about science and the environment are being published, and podcasts are spreading these creative ideas. This trend might inspire more people, both inside and outside science, to use poetry for learning and healing.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists and doctors are using poetry to explain science clearly and support emotional healing, creating a meaningful connection between facts and feelings.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques utilisent la poésie pour partager la science et soutenir la guérison
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certains scientifiques et médecins se tournent vers la poésie pour exprimer leurs émotions, expliquer des idées complexes et créer du lien avec les autres. Cette méthode combine science et art pour aider à comprendre et surmonter les expériences difficiles.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Danielle Chammas, médecin et chercheuse à l’Université de Californie à San Francisco, a écrit un poème après avoir dit au revoir à une patiente atteinte de cancer. Elle dirige aussi un programme où patients, soignants et personnels médicaux écrivent et partagent des poèmes pour aider à gérer le deuil et favoriser la guérison. D’autres scientifiques, comme la mathématicienne Colleen Farrelly et l’ingénieur Fionn Rogan, créent des poèmes inspirés par les tempêtes, l’énergie et la nature pour expliquer la science et sensibiliser au changement climatique.

**Impact** – La poésie offre aux scientifiques un nouveau moyen de communiquer des sujets compliqués comme les mathématiques ou le changement climatique de façon accessible et touchante. Elle aide aussi les médecins à soutenir émotionnellement leurs patients. Ce mélange de science et de poésie ouvre les esprits et les cœurs, rendant la science plus proche et encourageant une attention bienveillante.

**Prochaine étape** – Les programmes alliant poésie et science se développent, avec de plus en plus de chercheurs utilisant des poèmes pour partager leurs travaux et leurs émotions. Des recueils de poèmes sur la science et l’environnement sont publiés, et des podcasts diffusent ces idées créatives. Cette tendance pourrait inspirer davantage de personnes, dans et hors du milieu scientifique, à utiliser la poésie pour apprendre et guérir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques et médecins utilisent la poésie pour expliquer la science clairement et soutenir la guérison émotionnelle, créant un lien profond entre faits et sentiments.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos usan la poesía para compartir ciencia y apoyar la sanación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunos científicos y médicos recurren a la poesía para expresar emociones, explicar ideas complejas y conectar con otras personas. Esta combinación de ciencia y arte ayuda a comprender y enfrentar experiencias difíciles.

**Qué pasó** – Danielle Chammas, médica e investigadora en la Universidad de California en San Francisco, escribió un poema tras despedirse de una paciente con cáncer. También dirige un programa donde pacientes, cuidadores y personal médico escriben y comparten poemas para ayudar en el duelo y la sanación. Otros científicos, como la matemática Colleen Farrelly y el ingeniero Fionn Rogan, crean poemas inspirados en tormentas, energía y naturaleza para explicar la ciencia y crear conciencia sobre el cambio climático.

**Impacto** – La poesía ofrece a los científicos una forma nueva de comunicar temas difíciles como las matemáticas o el cambio climático de manera clara y emotiva. También ayuda a los médicos a apoyar a sus pacientes emocionalmente. Esta unión de ciencia y poesía abre mentes y corazones, acercando la ciencia y fomentando el cuidado.

**Próximo paso** – Los programas que combinan poesía y ciencia están creciendo, con más investigadores usando poemas para compartir su trabajo y sentimientos. Se publican colecciones de poemas sobre ciencia y medio ambiente, y podcasts difunden estas ideas creativas. Esta tendencia podría motivar a más personas dentro y fuera de la ciencia a usar la poesía para aprender y sanar.

**Frase clave** – Científicos y médicos usan la poesía para explicar la ciencia con claridad y apoyar la sanación emocional, creando una conexión significativa entre hechos y sentimientos.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, softly lit landscape featuring a lone autumn leaf gently detaching from a sturdy tree branch above a peaceful bedside scene symbolized by a glowing guitar silhouette, with swirling twin storm clouds dancing in harmony overhead and a winding path where a cyclist’s silhouette merges into elegant, flowing mathematical patterns resembling a topological shape, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and muted blues.
